metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = tunneling / exponential factor / the mechanism, CRIMSON = classical barrier / forbidden region / no tunneling classically
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no WKB connection-formula derivation, no Gamow-integral arccos, no Maslov-index detail
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Polonium-212 decays in a fraction of a microsecond. Thorium-232 takes longer than the age of the universe. Both decay by the exact same mechanism.

beats.narration_text: The same exponential that governs alpha decay also sets your USB flash memory, powers the Sun, and images individual atoms with a scanning tunneling microscope.

props.headline: The same exponential that governs alpha decay also sets your USB…

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The same exponential that governs alpha decay also sets your USB flash memory, powers the Sun, and images individual atoms with a scanning tunneling microscope.

beats.narration_text: One exponential controlling 24 orders of magnitude in half-life. And the same formula in a memory chip. How can one equation span so many scales and so many systems?

props.headline: One exponential controlling 24 orders of magnitude in half-life. And the…

card.headline: One exponential, 24 orders of magnitude.
card.dek: Alpha decay, flash memory, the Sun, the STM. How?

beats.narration_text: Classically, a particle with 5 MeV of energy cannot cross a 30 MeV barrier. It reflects off the wall. In quantum mechanics, the barrier has a finite width — and the wave function leaks through.

beats.narration_text: Inside the forbidden region the wave function doesn't oscillate — it decays exponentially. The thicker or higher the barrier, the more it decays before it can emerge on the far side.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Inside the forbidden region the wave function doesn't oscillate — it decays exponentially. The thicker or higher the barrier, the more it decays before it can e

beats.narration_text: The tunneling probability is e to the minus twice the Gamow integral — the integral of the momentum through the forbidden region divided by h-bar. Call it the Gamow factor.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The tunneling probability is e to the minus twice the Gamow integral — the integral of the momentum through the forbidden region divided by h-bar. Call it the G

beats.narration_text: Because the transmission sits inside an exponential, tiny changes in the barrier multiply into enormous changes in rate. Double the barrier width, and the probability doesn't halve — it drops by e to the minus a hundred.

beats.narration_text: A Coulomb barrier's width depends sharply on energy. A modest factor-of-two change in the alpha particle's energy changes the Gamow factor enough to swing the half-life by 24 orders of magnitude.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A Coulomb barrier's width depends sharply on energy. A modest factor-of-two change in the alpha particle's energy changes the Gamow factor enough to swing the h

beats.narration_text: Every application is the same Gamow factor in different clothing. Stellar fusion: protons tunnel through the Coulomb barrier at energies far too low to clear it. Flash memory: electrons tunnel through a thin oxide to trap charge.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Every application is the same Gamow factor in different clothing. Stellar fusion: protons tunnel through the Coulomb barrier at energies far too low to clear it

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: an electron with 1 eV of energy hits a 5 eV barrier that is 5 angstroms wide. The Gamow integral gives a decay constant of about 1 per angstrom. Over 5 angstroms, the tunneling probability is roughly e to the minus 10 — about 1 in 20,000.

beats.narration_text: One exponential governs every quantum tunneling process. Because the transmission lives inside an exponent, small changes in energy or width produce enormous changes in rate.
